svg.text: Two claims feel equally plausible until you compare the strength, relevance, and sufficiency of their support.
Start with the concrete case: Two claims feel equally plausible until you compare the strength, relevance, and sufficiency of their support.
Name the hidden structure: Support-strength rubric covering relevance, sufficiency, and alternative explanations.
Watch for the trap: Letting rhetoric substitute for evidential strength.
Transfer the habit immediately: Rank three arguments by inductive strength and explain your ranking.

Unlike broad intro courses, this lesson turns strong vs weak arguments into a practical operating skill through a real scenario, a visual model, a retrieval gate, and a transfer task built around argument mapping, evidence discipline, and fair-minded judgment.
Letting rhetoric substitute for evidential strength.

Rank three arguments by inductive strength and explain your ranking.
The goal is not to memorize the label, but to practice a repeatable move in real judgment. Ask the learner to produce a concrete artifact, not just an opinion.
